ExpertCommentary.ExpertCommentary:
At 0.37 in March 2020, Jocata Sumpoorn reflected a significant contraction in the sales activity of MSMEs, a result of economic activity shutting down - the national lockdown necessitated in response to the COVID-19 pandemic. The contraction was seen across the board in the IIP, with the overall index slumping by 18.7% over the previous year. The IHS Markit Composite PMI fell to 50.6 in March from 57.6 in February, making the lowest reading in five months; while the manufacturing index dropped to 51.8 from 54.5, the services sector reported a decline to 49.3 in March from 57.5 in February. New orders saw the fastest drop since September 2013 in fresh export contracts.

With the global economy in turmoil as transport and trade restrictions came into place to safeguard countries from the spread of the pandemic, India’s exports fell by 34.6% in March, while imports fell by 28.7%.

The unprecedented nature of the crisis and uncertainty led to strident demands for government support for small businesses, in particular.

After five months of contraction from March due to the severe restrictions of the national lockdown, Jocata Sumpoorn had shown marginal expansion at 0.53 in August and moderate expansion at 0.61 in September. The sales performance of credit-seeking MSMEs showed significant expansion in October, with the Jocata Sumpoorn at 0.66. The General IIP stood at 129.6, its highest level since the lockdown restrictions were put in place in April. As the IIP had gone through a phase of contraction for three months of August – October 2019, the overall growth of 4.5% in October was still muted and indicative of continuing stress in the mining and manufacturing sectors.

The responses through the survey based IHS Markit PMI showed significant strength, with the Composite PMI rising to 58.0 in October from 54.6 in September, making this the strongest increase in around nine years. The Manufacturing PMI rose to 58.9 in October from 56.8 in September, as purchasing managers reported the strongest pace of expansion in output in roughly 13 years. From its September level of 49.8, the Services PMI crossed into the expansionary zone for the first time since March and stood at 54.1 in October.

Even as the second wave of COVID-19 gripped the country and localized restrictions were in place, government support measures for MSMEs and the absence of severe national lockdown have enabled growth in sales activity for the sector. Jocata Sumpoorn stood at its highest level in a year at 0.79 in March, moving into the zone of substantial expansion in sales activity for credit-seeking MSMEs.

The General IIP Index also moved to a record high level of 145.6 in March, up from the 129.9 index of February. While the significantly high growth in IIP of 24.2% can partly be attributed to the low base effect from the previous year’s lockdown in March, all segments of the IIP recorded an increase over February, reflecting a recovery in the economy.

On the other hand, the responses through the survey based IHS Markit PMI showed a slight drop in March from the previous month: the Composite PMI stood at 56.0 in March, as against 57.3 in February; the Services PMI inched down to 54.6 in March from 55.3 in February; and the Manufacturing PMI moved from 57.5 in February to 55.4 in March. Despite the fall, the PMI stayed well above the threshold level of 50, reflecting an increase in business activity from the previous month.

The strong support given by the government as relief to MSMEs post-Covid continues to bolster the sector. After Jocata Sumpoorn showed substantial expansion with a level of 0.85 in April, the sales activity has been in the zones of moderate and signficant expansion. Since September, the IIP has been settling with the overall index coming off the high double-digit growth rates of the period March-August as the previous year's base effect runs out. Though growth in manufacturing IIP stood at 4.3% for September and 3.3% for October, there are fluctuations across sectors that reveal differential impact of the pandemic and the base effect – 13 out of 23 sectors reported growth in October e.g, textile manufacture posted 30%+ growth in September and October, while leather products showed continued stress with negative growth in both months.

The IHS Markit Purchasing Managers’ Index (PMI) survey reflected the revival in the economic activity as the Services PMI went up to 58.4 in October, its highest level in ten and a half years and Manufacturing PMI reported an eight-month high at 55.9; the Composite PMI consequently stood at 58.7, up from 55.3 in September, which was the strongest monthly expansion reported by the purchasing managers since January 2012. Overall, therefore the sentiment has been very positive for economic activity.

The Jocata Sumpoorn trajectory over the past year has in general shown the sales activity of credit-seeking MSMEs expanding at moderate to significant rates of expansion, except for one month of mild expansion in December 2021. Following a streak of three consecutive months of moderate expansion over the period September–November 2021, Jocata Sumpoorn showed mild expansion in December and once again moved into zones of moderate to significant expansion until May. This trend has been broadly in sync with the IIP, which also showed low rates of 1% growth in November and December 2021; the General IIP Index moved up from 134.5 in April to 137.8 in May, showing 19.7 percent growth over the previous year, despite the high base in May 2021. The Manufacturing IIP moved up to 134.6 in May from 131.6 in April, recording a 20.7% growth over the previous year. Except for the manufacture of pharmaceuticals, all other 22 sectors showed growth in May.

The S&P Global Composite PMI also moved up to 58.3 in May from 57.6 in April. The recovery was mainly in the services sector, where respondents reported the strongest rise in activity in 11 years; the index jumped to 58.9 in May from 57.9 in April, with further relaxations in the COVID-19 restrictions. The Manufacturing PMI stayed stable across the months, with a minor drop from 54.7 in April to 54.6 in May.

The sales performance of credit-seeking MSMEs showed a mild rate of expansion in October, after the Jocata Sumpoorn had recorded moderate expansion for four months since June 2022. The General IIP index has been showing some stress over the past four months, moving from 134.4 in July to a six-month low of 131.5 in August, a slight rise to 133.8 in September, and a drop again to 129.5 in October. General IIP contracted by 4.1% in October over the previous year, with manufacturing IIP contracting by 5.8%. After expanding for nineteen months, merchandise exports contracted by 12.1% in October over the previous year, while merchandise imports expanded by 10.0%.

There was a contrasting picture coming in from the S&P Global PMI, whose Composite Index reported at 55.5 in October, marginally higher than 55.1 in the previous month. However, the survey of purchasing managers of large firms showed that manufacturing production and new orders expanded at their slowest rate since June 2022.

Since June 2022, the Jocata Sumpoorn levels have shown that growth in the sales of credit-seeking MSMEs has settled in the zone of mild to moderate expansion. The index level moved up to 0.60 in January 2023 from the 0.59 level in December 2022. This is in line with the indications from the IIP, where the General IIP rose in January to 147.4, the third consecutive month of increase in production since the drop to 129.5 in October 2022. Growth in the General IIP stood at 5.8% over the previous year. Manufacturing IIP moved up to 145.5 in January from 144.9 in December, recording year-on-year growth of 4.5%. Analysis of the 23 sectors that make up the IIP showed that 13 sectors recorded growth over the previous year, while 10 sectors contracted.

In slight contrast, the responses from the survey-based S&P Global PMI showed lower expansion than December levels; the Composite PMI came in at 57.5 in January, down from December’s near 11-year high of 59.4. The Manufacturing PMI came in at 55.4 in January, down from December’s 57.8. Meanwhile, the Services PMI decreased to 57.2 in January (December: 58.5). In both sectors, output and new orders rose at softer rates than the previous month.

The sales activity of credit-seeking MSMEs has been exhibiting mild to moderate expansion since June 2022. Jocata Sumpoorn for February stands at the same level of 0.60 as the previous month, indicating steady, albeit mild, growth. On the IIP front, the General IIP dropped to 139.3 in February from 147.4 in January, growing at a rate of 6.0% over the previous year. The Manufacturing IIP fell from 145.5 in January to 137.6 in February, now almost at the same level as November 2022 and recording a growth of 5.9% over the previous year. 11 of the 23 sectors in the IIP contracted in February, while 12 recorded growth over the previous year.

The S&P Global Composite PMI came in at 59.0 in February, up from January’s 57.5, but this was essentially on the back of a strong services sector. Based on the responses of purchasing managers surveyed in the services sector, output and new orders grew at the fastest rate in 12 years, and the Services PMI rose to 59.4 in February from the previous month’s index of 57.2. On the other hand, the Manufacturing PMI softened marginally to 55.3 in February, slightly below January’s level of 55.4.

The current uptick in the sales performance of MSMEs into the zone of moderate expansion breaks the trend of mild expansion seen since December 2022. The General IIP moved up to 151.7 in March, reversing the drop seen in February to 139.3 from January’s level of 147.4, but growth in the General IIP over the previous year was a muted 1.9%. The Manufacturing IIP grew by 1.5%, while electricity output contracted in March. 13 of the 23 sectors that make up the IIP showed contractions over the previous year. Further, India’s exports moved to an eight-month high of USD 66.14 billion in March, but even at this value, exports of goods and services registered a decline of 7.53% over the previous year.

The picture from the PMI data also gave mixed signals: the S&P Global PMI Composite Index dropped marginally to 58.4 in March from 59 in February; the Services PMI fell from 59.4 in February to 57.8 in March; and the Manufacturing PMI moved to a three-month high from 55.3 in February to 56.4 in March. Purchasing managers of large firms in manufacturing reported the strongest growth in factory orders and production in three months. Meanwhile, after expanding at the fastest pace in 12 years in February, the firms surveyed in the service sector reported output and new orders growing at slightly weaker rates in March.

There are mixed signals coming in from the macro-economic data for April. Jocata Sumpoorn has been in a phase of moderate to mild expansion since June 2022 as the economy has thrown off the impact of the pandemic, but with the April 2023 index showing marginal expansion, this indicates the MSME sector is still to fully recover. The closure of the Emergency Credit Line Guarantee Scheme will have an impact on the sector that is credit-starved in the best of times.

The General IIP Index came in at 140.7, down from the 151.7 index value in March 2023, with growth at 4.6% over the previous year. The IIP Manufacturing index also dipped in April to 138.8, down from the high of 147.5 in March 2023, reporting growth of 5.5% over the previous year. Note that 12 out of 23 sectors contracted in April, when compared to the previous year. 

In contrast, the S&P Global Composite PMI Index rose significantly to 61.6 in April from 58.4 in March, now at the highest level since July 2010. Manufacturing PMI stood at a four month high in April at 57.2. The optimism was only tempered by price pressures as input costs rose at the fastest pace in three months.

Over the last three months, even as the Jocata Sumpoorn showed an expansion in the sales performance of credit-seeking MSMEs, the index has been slightly choppy, moving from 0.62 in March to 0.53 in April and recovering to 0.58 in May. Similar trends are seen in the IIP, the General IIP, which hit a high of 151.7 in March, dipped to 140.7 in April, and recovered in May to 145.6. The Manufacturing IIP hit its high of 147.5 in March, dipped in April to 138.8 and recovered in May to 142.4. The growth rates in May have been steady, with the General IIP growing at 5.3% and Manufacturing IIP up by 5.8% over the previous year. 10 out of the 23 sectors that make up the IIP contracted in May over the previous year’s levels. The exports of goods and services from India have been muted this year, with April-May registering lower than the previous year. This will clearly reflect in the performance of the MSMEs, which contribute more than 40% of India’s exports.

On the other hand, the responses from the S&P Global PMI showed bright optimism for India. The Composite PMI stayed at the 61.6 high in May, while the Manufacturing Index moved up from 57.2 in April to 58.7 in May, the strongest improvement since October 2020. The results from the survey of purchasing managers of large firms should reflect improved strength for the MSME sector down the value chain if demand persists.

Over the past year, Jocata Sumpoorn has shown a moderation in the expansion of sales activity among credit-seeking MSMEs. June was the second consecutive month of mild expansion. This is broadly in sync with the trends coming in from the Index of Industrial Production, where the General IIP moved down to 143.5 in June from its level of 145.6 in May. Manufacturing IIP has also moved down to 141.1 in June from 143.1 in May, recording year-on-year growth of 3.1% compared to 6.3% the previous month. 13 of the 23 sectors that make up the IIP contracted in June over their index the previous year. The S&P Global India Composite PMI fell to 59.4 in June 2023, down from the high 61.1 levels seen in April and May. In June, the survey of purchasing managers of large firms reported a slowdown in both factory activity and the service sector. The S&P Global India Manufacturing PMI fell to 57.8 in June 2023 from May's 31-month peak of 58.7.

The MSME sector does face constrained credit conditions, as the SIDBI-Transunion Pulse report for August 2023 has highlighted that over the quarter January-March 2023, credit demand grew by 33% while supply grew only by 11%. This will clearly impact the ability of firms to ramp up production and sales performance.

The latest official data releases show that growth estimates for the first quarter of 2023-24 is marginally below expectations; in real terms, GDP grew at 7.8% as against the RBI expectation of 8.0%. The main stress has come from the manufacturing sector, which grew by 4.7%. This appears to be in line with the indication coming in from Jocata Sumpoorn, which has been in a phase of marginal to mild expansion since April 2023. The non-oil and non-gold exports of merchandise also reflect a slowdown in activity, as their value has dropped from USD 110.72 over the period April-July 2022 to USD 102.71 in the period April-July 2023.

The General IIP for July stands at 142.5, lower than the June index of 143.5, while growth over the previous year is at 6.0%. 9 sectors of the 23 that make up the IIP recorded contractions over the previous year. Four sectors recorded a double-digit drop: the manufacture of apparel, furniture, wood and its products, computers, electronics, and optical products. The Manufacturing IIP was stable at 141.8 in July, compared to 141.1 in June, with growth of 5.0% over the previous year.

Meanwhile, the S&P Global Composite Purchasing Managers Index for India hit a high of 61.9 in July; this surge had more to do with the Services PMI rising to a 13 year high and the Manufacturing PMI falling to 57.7 in July from 57.8 in June and 58.7 in May.

Interestingly, though growth in outstanding non-food bank credit has increased in July to 19.8% year-on-year from 15.1% in July 2022, credit outstanding to MSMEs has expanded at a slower pace – 15.7% year-on-year compared to 21.5% in the previous year. At a sectoral level, year-on-year growth was the lowest for industry (5.8%), compared to all other sectors: agriculture (16.8%), services (23.1%), and personal loans (31.7%).

For the fourth consecutive month, the sales performance of credit-seeking MSMEs continued to grow at a rate of mild expansion in August. In his statement on October 6th, the RBI Governor flagged contracting global trade as a major risk for the economy going forward, even as domestic parameters are strong for India.

Data released on October 12 showed the General IIP moving up to 145.1 for August from 142.5 in July, registering a growth of 10.3% due to a low base, as the IIP had contracted in August 2022. Manufacturing IIP moved up to 143.5 from 141.8 in July, registering a growth of 9.3% in August. Just 7 out of the 23 sectors that make up the IIP contracted in August over last year's production levels, once again the impact of the previous year's low base coming into play.

The S&P Global India Composite PMI moved down to 60.9 in August from a high of 61.9 in July. While Manufacturing PMI moved to a three-month high of 58.6 in August from 57.7 in July, the Services PMI moved down to 60.1 in August from 62.3 recorded in July. It remains to be seen whether the high optimism recorded by the PMI survey of large firms has a positive impact on smaller firms down the value chain.

On the exports front, the non-petroleum and non-gems and jewellery export performance in August stood at USD 26.00 billion, compared to USD 25.20 billion in August 2022, indicating that the segment has picked up; however, for the period April-August, the performance has still not caught up with the same period last year.

For the fifth consecutive month, Jocata Sumpoorn showed the sales performance of the MSME sector in the zone of mild expansion, albeit at a higher level than August. India's macro-economic status has shown improved strength, despite the headwinds from global growth. Last month the International Monetary Fund (IMF) revised its growth projection for India for FY24 to 6.3% from its projection of 6.1% made in July 2023.

In its monthly Economic Survey, the Ministry of Finance has drawn attention to improved industrial activity as an emerging driver of growth, citing data from the IIP and RBI surveys. IIP recorded double-digit growth of 10.3% in August 2023, the highest in the last 14 months. There was also an improvement on the external trade front as data for September 2023 showed a moderation in exports by 1.2% while imports declined 13.7%, improving India's overall trade balance by 67.3 % on a YoY basis.

Interestingly, the non-petroleum non-gems and jewellery merchandise exports expanded by 1.8% while services exports expanded by 0.5% in September 2023, which is in line with the indications of mild expansion in MSME sales coming in from Jocata Sumpoorn.

The S&P Global Composite Purchasing Managers Index came in at 61.0 in September 2023, up from 60.9 in August, and this has been one of the highest readings in 13 years. However, Manufacturing PMI moved down to 57.5 in September from 58.6 the previous month, showing the softest pace since April, as output expanded the least in five months amid a slowdown in new orders. The purchasing managers of services firms reported an increase in new business and the Services PMI increased to 61.0 in September from 60.1 in August.

Jocata Sumpoorn moved up to 0.62 in October, showing sales activity for credit-seeking MSMEs in the zone of moderate expansion. As sales performance had been in the zone of mild expansion for five consecutive months,this rise could mark a breakout towards higher growth in the months ahead.

While access to capital has traditionally been a challenge for MSMEs, RBI data shows that MSMEs have been receiving a greater share of aggregate credit, at 14.5+ percent since January 2022, compared to 10-12 percent earlier. The growth rate of bank credit to MSMEs has also stabilised at steady double-digits post-pandemic, reported at 22.7 percent in October over the previous year. This augurs well for the sector, resulting in improved performance of MSME activity.

There have been some more bright spots in macro-economic data, especially on the exports front. Non-petroleum, non-gems, and jewellery exports rose by 11.74 percent from USD 21.99 Billion in October 2022 to USD 24.57 Billion in October 2023. Given that the MSME sector accounts for around 40 percent of India's exports, the Jocata-Sumpoorn move upwards in October is in line with the improvement in export performance data.

The latest national income data release for June-September 2023 shows the economy on a good footing, though there are still strong headwinds to weather. GVA in current prices grew at 9.0 percent year-on-year, and strong fixed investment and government consumption were key to the higher growth estimates this year. In terms of constant prices, GVA grew at 7.4 percent yoy, compared to 5.4 percent yoy in June-September 2022. Manufacturing GVA took the lead amongst all sectors, with growth at 13.9 percent. While part of the surge in manufacturing GVA can be explained by the low base (-3.8 percent yoy growth in June-September 2022), lower commodity prices have also played a role in better performance. However, moving ahead, in recent months, the S&P Global PMI Manufacturing index has indicated a moderation, as the index stood at 56 in November and 55.5 in October, compared to its level of 58.6 in August.

One cautionary note is coming through the slow growth in private consumption in the national income estimates, which aligns with the mild expansion of sales activity of the MSME sector as shown through the Sumpoorn index in the June-September quarter. The IIP reports lower growth in consumer durables and non-durables in the June-September quarter, while primary goods, capital goods and infrastructure goods have seen double-digit growth. With uneven monsoons and large-scale crop damage, rural consumption has been hit, and demand for jobs under MGNREGA in September and October has been10-18 percent higher than in the corresponding months last year and 26-31 percent higher than the pre-COVID period. So even as this year's festive season has recorded high sales in many categories of vehicles, tractor sales are still lower than last year, indicating some stress in rural India.

One more challenge ahead for MSMEs is that firms may have to contend with the fallout from RBI's recent measures to tighten risk weight norms for unsecured retail loans. While the move will make for healthier balance sheets and possibly higher allocation of credit towards the MSME sector, the impact on overall capital available with NBFCs as well as demand for consumer durables remains to be seen.

Jocata Sumpoorn moved down to 0.53 in November indicating that the sales performance of credit-seeking MSMEs is at the level last seen in April 2023, down from the relatively higher zone of moderate expansion in October. A number of macro-indicators showed a similar trend. While bank credit to MSMEs continues to remain robust, the RBI press release on sectoral deployment of bank credit in November called out the "tapered down" growth in credit to NBFCs, which is expected to continue in response to the RBI measures tightening risk-weight norms. The RBI's latest Financial Stability Report for the second quarter of 2023-24 revealed a mixed picture - on one hand, the quality of bank MSME portfolios improved with the GNPA declining to 4.7% in September 2023 from 6.8% in March 2023 and 7.7% in September 2022 and on the other hand, SMA-2 loans (that is loans whose payments are overdue for 61 to 90 days) rose slightly to 1.7% in September 2023 from 0.9% in March 2023, showing some stress in the system.

The bright spot in export data also dimmed a bit in November - Non-petroleum and non-gems and jewellery exports which had risen by 11.74% in October, fell by 2.8% year on year in November. According to a recent reply in Parliament, the MSME sector accounted for 45.56% of India's exports in the first six months of this financial year, higher than its 43.59% share in 2022-23. The Jocata Sumpoorn therefore aligns with the export trend relatively closer.

Further, the S&P Global Composite PMI for India slipped to 57.4 in November from 58.4 the previous month - while Manufacturing PMI was marginally up at 56, compared to 55.5 in October, the Service PMI fell from 58.4 in October to 56.9 in November. The RBI's Financial Stability Report noted that while the overall GNPA of loans extended under the ECLGS rose to 6.5% in September 2023 from 5.5% in March 2023, services and trade loans that made up a third of the ECLGS disbursements, accounted for nearly half of the total delinquencies. Clearly, the impact of the pandemic is still flowing through the system and some sectors are more affected than others. So while the Indian economy continues to be on a strong footing, data from the Jocata Sumpoorn index over the year showing growth in the sales activity of MSMEs at a steady yet slow pace, makes the case for more targeted government support for the MSME sector.

MonthlyCommentary.comment: Jocata Sumpoorn moved down to 0.37 in March from 0.55 in February, entering the zone of significant contraction in the sales performance of credit-seeking MSMEs.
Jocata Sumpoorn moved up to 0.66 in October 2020; this is the first significant expansion in the sales performance of credit-seeking MSMEs in 2020 as COVID-19 showed signs of subsidence in India.
Jocata Sumpoorn came in at a high of 0.79 in March 2021, the first substantial expansion in the sales performance of credit-seeking MSMEs in more than a year.
Jocata Sumpoorn moved down to 0.64 in October from 0.65 in the previous month. The sales performance of credit-seeking MSMEs is in the zone of moderate expansion for two consecutive months.
Jocata Sumpoorn moved up to 0.73 in May 2022. The sales performance of credit-seeking MSMEs is in a phase of significant expansion for two consecutive months.
Jocata Sumpoorn moved down to 0.56 in October, indicating mild expansion in the sales activity of credit-seeking MSMEs.
Jocata Sumpoorn stood at 0.60 in January 2023, indicating a mild expansion in the sales performance of credit-seeking MSMEs.
Jocata Sumpoorn stayed in the zone of mild expansion in February 2023, standing at 0.60, the same level as seen in January.
Jocata Sumpoorn moved up to 0.62 in March, indicating a moderate expansion in the sales activity of credit-seeking MSMEs.
Jocata Sumpoorn moved down to 0.53 in April 2023. Though the sales performance of credit-seeking MSMEs stays in the expansion zone, the growth is marginal this month.
Jocata Sumpoorn came in at 0.58 for the month of May, signalling mild expansion in the sales invoices for credit seeking MSMEs in India.
Jocata Sumpoorn moved down to 0.55 in June from May’s level of 0.58. The sales performance of credit seeking MSMEs is in the zone of mild expansion.
Jocata Sumpoorn moved to 0.57 in July from its 0.55 level in June. Sales activity for credit-seeking MSMEs has continued in the phase of mild expansion since May.
Jocata Sumpoorn moved down to 0.55 in August from its 0.57 level in July. Sales activity for credit-seeking MSMEs has continued in the phase of mild expansion since May.
Jocata Sumpoorn moved up to 0.58 in September from 0.55 in August, staying in the zone of mild expansion of sales activity.
Jocata Sumpoorn moved up to 0.62 in October from the level of 0.58 in September, moving into the zone of moderate expansion.
Jocata Sumpoorn stood at 0.53 in November, moving down from the level of 0.62 in October, indicating that sales activity of MSMEs is in the phase of marginal expansion.
Jocata Sumpoorn stood at 0.57 in December, moving up from the level of 0.53 in November, indicating that sales activity of MSMEs is in the phase of mild expansion.
